source.title: Theta Oscillations Support Prefrontal-hippocampal Interactions in Sequential Working Memory

confidence_rationale: This human SEEG study demonstrates that theta oscillations (3-10 Hz) carry DLPFC goal-context signals into hippocampal sequential processing, with directed DLPFC-to-hippocampus theta influence selectively enhanced when sequential working memory load is high and memory precision is better. The study is indirect support for MECH-128: it establishes that prefrontal goal-context information is actively routed into hippocampal sequential processing via theta-band phase coupling and Granger causality, consistent with the claim that predictive sequence generation requires goal-context conditioning. Confidence is moderate because the study measures working memory for abstract ordered sequences (not trajectory prediction), and the theta coupling is a correlate of PFC-hippocampal communication rather than direct evidence that PFC goal context shapes trajectory generation specifically.
failure_signatures: The task (ordering lines clockwise) involves sequential working memory but not spatial navigation or predictive trajectory generation; the generalisation to E1 trajectory prediction is inferential.
Theta phase coherence and Granger causality measure PFC-hippocampal communication direction but do not directly show that goal content from DLPFC shapes the predictions generated by hippocampus.
The study uses patients with epilepsy, raising concerns about generalisability of theta dynamics to healthy brains.

The coupling enhancement is for memory precision (recall accuracy) not for trajectory generation quality -- the link to MECH-128's claim about trajectory shaping requires an additional inferential step.

mapping.source_claim_statement: DLPFC-to-hippocampus theta-band directional influence (Granger causality) is selectively enhanced during sequential working memory ordering relative to maintenance, particularly for trials with higher memory precision; theta phase coherence between DLPFC and hippocampus correlates with memory accuracy.
mapping.ree_translation: DLPFC represents goal/task context in working memory. The selective enhancement of DLPFC-to-hippocampus theta influence during the ordering condition (which requires holding and manipulating a sequence with respect to a goal structure) supports the claim that when goal-context must actively shape sequential processing (as MECH-128 requires for E1), prefrontal goal representations are actively projected into hippocampal computation via theta coupling. This is the biological signature of what MECH-128 formalises as z_goal_latent injected into E1's LSTM at each recurrent step.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The biological mechanism is theta-band phase coupling between separate brain regions, which is architecturally distant from concatenating a goal latent to an LSTM input. The study does not show that PFC goal content determines the content of hippocampal predictions, only that PFC drives hippocampal activation more strongly under goal-relevant sequential conditions. The direction of causal influence (PFC to hippocampus) is correct for MECH-128, but the mechanism remains indirect.
mapping.source_context: Human SEEG; epilepsy patients; dorsolateral prefrontal cortex and hippocampus; sequential working memory (line ordering task); theta oscillations, phase coherence, Granger causality

confidence_components.notes: Neuroscience Bulletin, peer-reviewed, human intracranial recordings are high-quality data. Mapping fidelity is moderate -- the paper supports the right causal direction (PFC conditions hippocampal sequential processing) but does not directly test trajectory prediction. Transfer risk elevated by task distance from trajectory generation, patient population, and the gap between LFP coupling and LSTM architecture.
